entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: STEWARD OF THE MYSTERIES.
entries.body: There is a big difference between being a servant of Christ and a steward of the mysteries of God. Anyone who has read the Bible can be a servant of Christ, but to be a steward of the mysteries one must plumb their depths. Paul was acting as a steward of the mysteries when he commissioned Luke, for example, to write his Gospel, and when he sent Timothy{fn:0} to sort out the Ephesian church. I would even dare to say that in Corinth Paul acted like a servant of Christ, whereas in Ephesus he became a steward of the mysteries of God.{fn:1} {fn:2}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.18.10-16

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: IN ACCORD WITH THE APOSTLES.
entries.body: Paul says this because some of the Corinthians were denigrating him. He did not preach anything different from the apostles. By calling himself a servant of Christ and a steward of the mysteries of God, Paul implicitly points out who the false apostles are. He denies that what they preach is of Christ, because it is not in accord with apostolic tradition. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: MYSTERIES NOT GIVEN INDISCRIMINATELY.
entries.body: Paul honors the Corinthians by calling them servants and makes this even more precise when he adds the term stewards. For we should not give the mysteries of God indiscriminately to everyone, but only to those to whom they are due and to whom it is right that we should minister. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 10.5

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: TRUSTWORTHY STEWARDS.
entries.body: If Paul can say this of people like himself, Peter and Apollos, how much more will it be true of us? We ought to be on our guard to make sure that we are found to be trustworthy stewards. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.18.25-27

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: ENTRUSTED BY THE MASTER.
entries.body: A steward’s duty is to administer well the things that have been entrusted to him. The things of the master’s are not the stewards but the reverse—what is his really belongs to his master. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 10.5

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: WHAT YOU HAVE IS FROM GOD.
entries.body: As you receive everything, call upon God for everything. What you have is from God. Always acknowledge that you are his debtor. {fn:0}
entries.citation: On Theodosius 22

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: SAFEKEEPING THE DEPOSIT.
entries.body: No banker plays fast and loose with other people’s deposits. Rather he looks after them in order to keep them safe for the one who has entrusted them to him. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 188

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: HIS CLEAR CONSCIENCE.
entries.body: It is obvious that Paul was not worried about himself because he had a clear conscience. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: HIS RELUCTANCE TO JUDGE HIMSELF.
entries.body: Paul says this not to exalt himself but to humble others who were getting too full of themselves. Paul says that he would not even presume to judge himself, because he is not capable of making an adequate assessment. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 9.2

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: EXAGGERATION AND CONDEMNATION.
entries.body: Paul accuses the Corinthians of two things. First, they exaggerate their praise, and second, they condemn others when they have no right to judge. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 186

entries.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch
entries.title: NOT ACQUITTED.
entries.body: Through the wrongdoings of others I become a better disciple, but I am not thereby acquitted. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Epistle to the Romans 5

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: UNAWARE OF ANYTHING AGAINST HIMSELF.
entries.body: Paul knew that even if his heart was still prone to sin, his deeds were upright. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.18.49-51

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: BLAMING UNREASONABLY.
entries.body: Paul may have committed certain sins without knowing that they are sins. His purpose here is not to say that he is blameless but to stop the mouths of those who were blaming him unreasonably. God is our judge, because only he knows for sure what is going on in our hearts. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 9.3

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: UNABLE TO JUDGE MYSELF.
entries.body: If I am unable to judge myself, how shall I presume to judge others? {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 187

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: WHAT REACHES GOD’S EARS.
entries.body: Why does Paul mention only commendation from God and say nothing about condemnation? The reason seems to be that only that which is commendable will reach God’s ears; the rest will be passed over in silence. I would even go so far as to say that it is God who receives the commendable things we have done, whereas the rest goes straight to the devil. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.18.106-12

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: DO NOT PRONOUNCE JUDGMENT BEFORE THE TIME.
entries.body: God will judge in his own good time. A judge is insulted if a servant presumes to pronounce a verdict before the judge makes the decision known. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: GOD KNOWS OUR HEARTS.
entries.body: Paul is not talking here about those sins that we all recognize and confess as such. Rather he is speaking about preferring one person before another and making invidious comparisons of moral behaviors. Only God, who knows all our secret doings, can judge that sort of thing with accuracy. Only he knows what is more and what is less worthy of punishment. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 9.3

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: APPLYING TACT.
entries.body: As long as there was a need for expressions as harsh as these, Paul refrained from drawing up the curtain and went on arguing as if he himself were the person to whom they were addressed. But when the time came to be gentler, he tore the curtain away and revealed who the people were whom he had concealed under the names of Paul and Apollos. This was not hypocrisy but gentleness and tact. For if he had said openly that the men whom the Corinthians were attacking were saints, they might have taken it badly. But by first humbling himself he gained their attention and respect. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 9.1

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: EVERYTHING IS A GIFT.
entries.body: Paul wrote this to people who thought that it was better to be baptized by some people rather than by others, who had been led astray by their eloquence and who by some trick believed that perverse teachings were right. In fact, everything these people had they had received from the apostle. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: YOU HAVE RECEIVED.
entries.body: By showing that they have received all that they have from someone else, Paul points out the deficiencies of the Corinthians, which were not few in number. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 9.3-4

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: BOASTING AS IF IT WERE NOT A GIFT.
entries.body: The people who boast imagine that they are justified by their own efforts, and therefore they glory in themselves, not in the Lord. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Letter to Valentine

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE REPRIMAND.
entries.body: Arguments like these, which appeal to our sense of shame, have two advantages. On the one hand, they cut deeper than open invective would ever do. On the other hand, they cause the person reprimanded to bear that deeper wound with greater patience. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 12.4

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: A SPECTACLE.
entries.body: Paul was worthy to be watched by angels as he strove to win the prize of Christ, as he struggled to establish the life of angels on earth and confound the wickedness of angels in heaven. For he wrestled with spiritual wickedness. Rightly did the world watch him, to follow his example. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Episcopal Election at Vercellae 63.71

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE CONTRAST WITH PETTINESS.
entries.body: From the things by which he vilifies himself, Paul shows us how great he is. From the things that make the Corinthians proud he displays their littleness. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 12.6

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: WHY THE ANGELS MARVEL.
entries.body: The angels marvel at the apostles’ fortitude. As for human beings, some rejoice in the apostles’ afflictions, while others are moved to pity but have no help to offer. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 189

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: FOOLS FOR CHRIST.
entries.body: Those who love Christ are fools as far as the world is concerned. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: HELD IN DISREPUTE.
entries.body: Paul said these things in order to provoke the Corinthians to consider that they should zealously seek to emulate the apostles in their dangers and their indignities, not in their honors and glories. For it is the former, not the latter, that the gospel requires. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 13.1

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE CONSTANT RISK OF THE ATHLETE.
entries.body: Paul is talking about the present as much as about the past, because the Christian must always be living in this way, and not just occasionally. The wrestler may be crowned after a single victory, but if he then goes on to lose, he will not be crowned a second time. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 13.2

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: SUFFERING WITHOUT ANGER.
entries.body: Paul is saying that the main point is not that he and his fellow apostles are suffering, for that is common to all. What is unique about them is that they are suffering without despair or anger. On the contrary, they are full of rejoicing, and they prove it by returning good for the evil they receive. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 13.2

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: SILENCE, NOT REPROACH.
entries.body: Christ commanded us to bear insults meekly, both so that we might grow in virtue ourselves and that we might put our adversaries to shame.{fn:0} That effect is best produced not by reproach but by silence. {fn:1}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 13.2

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE PHYSICIAN’S WORK.
entries.body: Paul is acting here like a good physician who alleviates the pain caused by his operation to remove the disease, so that the sick person will let himself be cured. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE KNIFE AND THE PAIN.
entries.body: Not to speak against sins would have been impossible, since they would have remained uncorrected. To have left the wound untended after having spoken would have been harsh. Therefore Paul apologizes for being severe, because so far from destroying the effect of the knife it makes it sink in even deeper, while at the same time it looks toward soothing the pain of the wound. When a person is told that these things are being said in love and not in reproach, he will be more open to receiving correction. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 13.3

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: DISTINGUISHING THE TRUE FATHER FROM SUBSEQUENT GUIDES.
entries.body: The father is the one who has sown the seed of the gospel in their souls. The guides are those who have taken the child later on and helped him develop. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.21.9-11

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: I BECAME YOUR FATHER.
entries.body: Paul is telling the Corinthians that nobody else will ever love them the way he does. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE LOVE OF A FATHER.
entries.body: Paul is not claiming any dignity here, but rather he is showing the depth of his love. Dignity belongs to the teacher, but love is the mark of the father. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 13.4

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: BE HUMBLE.
entries.body: Paul is saying to the Corinthians: Be humble as I am humble, suffer as I suffer. It is by your sufferings, not by your gifts, that you will be rated. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.21.12-14

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: IMITATE PAUL.
entries.body: Paul wants them to be imitators of him in these things, so that just as he has endured many hardships from unbelievers for their salvation and is still doing so as long as he preaches the free gift of God’s grace day and night, so they too ought to remain in his faith and doctrine and not accept the evil teachings of false apostles. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: IMITATE CHRIST.
entries.body: Paul’s real aim was that the Corinthians should imitate Christ. But because of their weakness, he presents himself as an intermediate model to follow. It is only because he imitates Christ that he exhorts the people to imitate him. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 13.5

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: BE PREPARED TO SUFFER.
entries.body: By this Paul meant that if he was humble, they should also be humble. What he was prepared to suffer, they should be prepared to suffer. They were to glory in their hardships, not in their blessings. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 190

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: SENDING TIMOTHY TO THEM.
entries.body: Paul shows how much he loves the Corinthians by sending his dearly beloved child, Timothy, to them, choosing thereby to be separated from him for the Corinthians’ sake. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 14.1

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: MY WAYS IN CHRIST.
entries.body: By ways in Christ Paul means his embodied good deeds. He tells the Corinthians to remember them, because they are self-evident and do not need to be taught. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.21.20-22

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: TEACHING AND LIFE.
entries.body: Paul’s behavior was completely consistent with his teaching. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 4

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE CORINTHIANS’ PRIDE.
entries.body: Some of the Corinthians were angry that Paul had not come to them, not because they wanted him to but because they were proud and imagined that Paul thought they were unworthy of a visit. In fact, Paul wanted to go but had more important things to do. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: SOME THOUGHT HIM WEAK.
entries.body: It is clear from this that what was being said was that Paul and his companions were weak, while the Corinthians were strong.
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PAUL PROMISES TO COME.
entries.body: Paul couches his promise to come in the will of God, because God knows more than man. If there was some advantage in Paul’s going to Corinth, God would make it known, and if he did not turn up, the Corinthians would know that the Lord had not wanted him to. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on Paul’s Epistles

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: FOR CORRECTION.
entries.body: Paul promises to come, so that the Corinthians can prepare themselves for correction. On the one hand he was forced by his irritation to say I shall come, but on the other hand he added if the Lord wills, because of his sense of dependence on God. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE KINGDOM OF GOD AND POWER.
entries.body: It is not smooth talk that reveals the presence of the kingdom of God, but power. When there is power in the words, then the kingdom is present in them. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.22.6-8

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: NO HIDING BEHIND A WALL OF WORDS.
entries.body: Actions speak louder than words, says Paul. If those Corinthians who are now arrogant want to prove something, let them show me when I come whether they can do the same miracles I can do. I do not want to find them hiding behind a wall of words, for that sort of thing means nothing to me. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 14.2

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: NOT PREACHING BUT BEHAVIOR.
entries.body: It is not enough to preach the kingdom of God in order to be saved; one must also behave in a way which is worthy of the kingdom. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 191

entries.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
entries.title: NO FALSE SECURITY.
entries.body: Let us not deceive ourselves with a false security, believing that a nonresponsive faith lacking good works can deliver us against the day of judgment. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Sermon 209.3

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE CHOICE.
entries.body: Paul leaves it up to the Corinthians to decide how he should come to them. We too have a choice. Either we can fall into hell, or we can obtain the kingdom. But if you say that you are willing to do the right thing but not able, you are mistaken. All that means is that you are not willing strongly enough. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 14.5

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: LOVE HIDDEN IN THE ROD.
entries.body: The rod does not mean that there is no love, but love is hidden behind its blows and is not perceived by the one who is on the receiving end. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on 1 Corinthians 2.23.6-8

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: FIRST THE ROD, THEN GENTLENESS.
entries.body: Paul mentioned a rod first, that he might afterward comfort them with the spirit of meekness. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Synagogue at Callinicum 41.4

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: THE POWER OF THE SPIRIT.
entries.body: By rod Paul meant the compelling power of the Spirit, which he had used against Elymas and which God had used against him.{fn:0} {fn:1}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church
